title: Module Check: The Interview Algorithm Landscape

questions.question: Name the 6 steps of the UMPIRE framework in order and state what happens at each step.
questions.answer: Understand (clarify the problem and constraints), Match (identify the pattern), Plan (outline the algorithm in pseudocode), Implement (write code), Review (trace through examples), Evaluate (state time/space complexity).

questions.question: What property must an input have for binary search to be applicable, and why does that property matter?
questions.answer: The input must be sorted (or have a monotonic property). This allows each comparison to eliminate half the remaining search space, achieving O(log n) instead of O(n).

questions.question: At which UMPIRE step should you write the first line of code, and what three steps must precede it?
questions.answer: I (Implement) — only after completing U (Understand), M (Match), and P (Plan). Writing code before planning leads to wasted effort on the wrong algorithm.

questions.question: According to the Pareto principle applied to algorithm interviews, approximately what share of problems can be solved with the top 20% of patterns?
questions.explanation: The Pareto principle (80/20 rule) holds here: mastering hash maps, two pointers, BFS/DFS, binary search, and DP covers the large majority of interview problems. 50% and 65% understate the leverage; 95% overstates it.

questions.question: During the Review step of UMPIRE, what is the primary goal?

options.A: Refactor the code to use fewer lines
options.B: Trace through the example inputs to verify correctness before the interviewer asks
options.C: Look up standard library functions you may have missed
options.D: Restate the problem to confirm you understood it

questions.explanation: Review means manually tracing your implementation against the given examples to catch off-by-one errors or missed edge cases. Refactoring and library lookup are secondary concerns; problem restatement belongs in the Understand step.
